design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect weapon power-ups ('wings') to equip onto ship, navigate dual-layer levels (air/ground), defeat stage bosses (Gobunasu), progress through 30 levels with horizontally infinite scrolling sections
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Weapon management—equipped wings can be damaged by enemy fire and must be replaced; strategic choice between maintaining current loadout or ejecting for better options; dual-layer navigation adds positioning complexity
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Piloting an adaptable robotic aircraft (FX-1) that transforms via modular weapon systems to overcome different enemy formations and boss encounters
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Famicom version diverges significantly from arcade prototype with altered power-up mechanics, enemy patterns, and newly introduced weapons (Hammer, Dyna, Fire); adds rapid-fire capability and hidden items that arcade lacks. This suggests deliberate redesign philosophy prioritizing home console accessibility and item discovery incentives over strict arcade port fidelity

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives system (quantity unspecified); loss occurs via enemy attacks or obstacle collisions
difficulty.continues_system: Continue option available on title screen (replaced by stage select in debug mode)
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; no mid-level checkpoints documented. Horizontal scrolling sections allow infinite looping, suggesting potential for resource attrition
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Significant agency through weapon selection and ground-level evasion mechanic—player can press secondary button to descend and become temporarily invulnerable to airborne attacks while on ground level, creating skill-based avoidance option. Ground level creates new vulnerability to ground-unit attacks, forcing tactical layer-switching

progression.upgrade_system: Wing-based modular weapon system—7 weapons in arcade version, 11+ in Famicom (Cannon, Wide, Multi, Van, Side, Anti, Jump, Hammer, Dyna, Fire, plus 3 hidden special weapons: Sterling-Silver, Aurora-Harrier, Oct-Blaster). Weapons attach to ship sides and can be damaged/ejected mid-stage. Voltage item allows spare weapon storage usable via pause menu
progression.unlock_mechanism: Wings collected as power-ups during level progression; hidden weapons discoverable by destroying specific obstacle structures; Comet item allows skipping 4 levels ahead at point cost
replayability_hooks: 30 levels with warps to alternate paths (Famicom addition)
Hidden items scattered under obstacles encourage exploratory replay
Multiple weapon configurations per stage encourage experimentation
Comet skip mechanic creates speedrun-adjacent progression variants

world_building_techniques: Enemy naming convention (Gobunasu as stage bosses) provides minimal thematic context
Robotic aircraft designation (FX-1) suggests sci-fi military setting inferred from visual design only
No cutscenes, dialogue, or plot exposition; world-building purely environmental/visual

boss_design.philosophy: Stage-terminating encounters with entity name 'Gobunasu'; no documented variations in boss behavior across stages or adaptive difficulty mechanics
notable_bosses: Gobunasu (generic designation for all stage bosses; specific boss names and patterns not documented)

faction_indicators: Military aesthetics (robotic aircraft vs. enemy forces)
No faction narrative; purely antagonistic framing
danger_signals: Enemy bosses (Gobunasu) present mandatory combat gates
Weapon degradation under fire creates resource scarcity tension
Ground-level vulnerability to ground units creates layered threat assessment
environmental_storytelling: Obstacle structures that conceal items imply tactical terrain design
Dual-layer (air/ground) geography suggests environment-based strategy rather than narrative world-building
Infinite horizontal scrolling in sections suggests expansive sci-fi battlefields

sources_used: tcrf: B-Wings (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor (debug mode, level select, developmental context)
wikipedia: B-Wings (gameplay mechanics, weapon systems, level design, Famicom version distinctions from arcade)
